game_name: Dune II: The Building of a Dynasty

danger_signals.visual_form: Gigantic sandworm entity appearing on sand terrain
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental threat that swallows vehicles and infantry whole; blocks player progression and destroys units

danger_signals.visual_form: Bumps/raised terrain features on sand tiles
danger_signals.meaning: Concealed spice that triggers destructive explosion when shot or driven over; unit destruction risk

danger_signals.signal: Enemy unit presence in fog of war
danger_signals.meaning: Unknown enemy positions; potential for surprise attack

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual decay of buildings over time on sand without concrete foundations
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental attrition; resource drain if structures not maintained

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy structures and defensive installations visible on cleared map
danger_signals.meaning: Active enemy presence; direct military threat requiring destruction or capture

safety_signals.visual_form: Rocky/stone ground tiles distinct from sand
safety_signals.meaning: Safe building zone; blocks sandworm traversal; foundation for player base
Reduced structure decay; economic efficiency; structural stability

safety_signals.visual_form: Visible map area previously shrouded in darkness
safety_signals.meaning: Scouted safe zone; known enemy positions; strategic information advantage
Active resource collection; economic growth; functional spice operation

safety_signals.visual_form: Refinery structure linked by visual path/proximity to harvest operations
safety_signals.meaning: Spice conversion to credits; economic viability; base production active

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Atreides faction symbol (not detailed in sources)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Sonic Tank (unique heavy armor unit); Atreides-style trikes (fast reconnaissance units)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Atreides base structures and unit designs; mission briefings and endgame cutscenes reflect Atreides worldview

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Harkonnen faction symbol (not detailed in sources)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Devastator tank (heavy armor, superior ordnance); Quad light attack vehicle instead of trikes; Harkonnen-specific weaponry
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Harkonnen base architecture; heavy vehicle-focused unit roster; mission briefings reflect Harkonnen worldview and objectives

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Ordos faction symbol (not detailed in sources)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Deviator tank (fires nerve gas to temporarily flip unit allegiance); Raider trike (faster than Atreides equivalent); Ordos-specific unit silhouettes
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Ordos base structures and visual identity; non-canon status reflected in unique unit roster; mission briefings reflect Ordos worldview

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Elite heavy infantry units; unplayable faction appearing in endgame alliance stage
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Appears only in final mission when player dominates map; visual representation of imperial authority and military intervention

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Early game - unoccupied territory, no enemy presence
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Empty base terrain with spice fields visible; minimal enemy structures; fog of war covers most map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Focus on establishing base, harvesting initial spice, building basic defense; no immediate combat pressure

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mid game - territorial conflict between Houses
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple faction base structures visible on map; spice fields marked with orange coloration (darker orange = higher concentration); enemy units moving across terrain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Shift from passive harvesting to active military engagements; territory conquest missions; multi-front defense requirements

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Late game - player dominance on world map
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Two remaining enemy Houses visually allied; combined base structures; player's territory heavily developed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Endgame cutscenes trigger (faction-specific); missions focus on destroying consolidated enemy forces; Sardaukar units appear as intervention force

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Orange coloration on sand terrain with intensity variation (darker = richer deposits)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player prioritizes harvester deployment to high-concentration zones; resource acquisition strategy driven by visual richness indicators

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fog of war (darkness) obscures terrain, enemy positions, and resource availability
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Scout deployment required; unit exploration removes fog permanently; strategic planning limited until full map intelligence gathered

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: New building types available; upgraded unit variants appear in production queue; faction-specific super weapons become constructible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Access to improved technology and higher-order weaponry based on mission completion; varied gameplay enforced through unit restrictions per House

environmental_storytelling.example: Orange-colored sand indicates resource availability; darker orange signals high concentration; communicates economic opportunity and relative wealth of territories without text
Rocky terrain vs. sand visually communicates buildable vs. unbuildable zones; terrain choice tells story of player base survival and adaptation to Arrakis
Gigantic worm entity visually communicates planetary danger; rocky terrain blocking worm travel tells environmental story of Arrakis ecology
Visible deterioration of buildings on sand without concrete indicates harsh environmental attrition; communicates resource cost of survival on Arrakis
Introduction and endgame cutscenes vary by House faction; visual storytelling communicates disparate worldviews and political motivations without gameplay changes
Each House's unique unit types (Sonic Tank for Atreides, Devastator for Harkonnen, Deviator for Ordos) tell visual story of military philosophy and technological capability

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves to orange-colored terrain; collects resources over time; converts spice to credits when reaching refinery; indicates economic health and player activity
Roams sand areas swallowing vehicles and infantry; blocked by rocky terrain; indicates environmental threat independent of faction conflict

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Visible in cleared fog of war
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Patrol base perimeter; respond to player incursion; attack player structures and units; behavior intensity increases as player advances territory
Decay over time (faster without concrete); generate units when provided credits; become targets for enemy attack; decay rate communicates maintenance burden
Explode when shot or driven over; destroy units; disappear and become standard spice field; indicate hidden danger and resource volatility
Allows production of captured House's units; grants access to enemy unit types; indicates tactical advantage through structure capture mechanic

notes: Dune II pioneered the RTS genre format including visual communication templates still used in modern strategy games. The game uses color (orange spice fields), terrain type (rocky vs. sand), and visual decay to communicate world-state without explicit UI elements. Faction identity is expressed through unit roster uniqueness rather than color-coding. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on Arrakis ecology (sandworms, spice, terrain) as narrative backdrop to human political conflict. The fog of war permanent reveal system (once explored, stays visible) is a key visual-strategic design choice differentiating Dune II from later RTS iterations.
